title: The Story of Yusuf: The Most Beautiful Story

captions.youtube:
The Story of Yusuf is the only chapter in the Qur'an that tells a single story from beginning to end, and Allah Himself calls it the most beautiful of stories (12:3). This is a faithful, fully sourced retelling of Surah Yusuf, with the lesser-known authentic details most retellings leave out: his lineage of four prophets in a row (Sahih al-Bukhari), that he was given half of all beauty (Sahih Muslim), his call to tawhid inside the prison (12:39), his refusal to leave prison until his name was cleared and the Prophet's words about that patience (Sahih al-Bukhari), Ya'qub's grief (12:84-86), the scent of Yusuf (12:94), and the way the Prophet echoed 'no blame upon you today' at the Conquest of Makkah. Seven verses are shown on screen with translation. Nothing is embellished; every claim is on-screen sourced to the Qur'an or to Sahih al-Bukhari and Sahih Muslim.

Sources: Surah Yusuf (Qur'an 12), text from Quran.com; Sahih al-Bukhari (3390 lineage; 3372 his patience in prison); Sahih Muslim (162, given half of beauty); the Conquest of Makkah from the books of seerah. Arabic is shown with translation, never recited or synthesized.

captions.shorts: Betrayed, enslaved, imprisoned, and every step was carrying him exactly where Allah meant him to be. The Story of Yusuf.

_note: Long-form 16:9 (~12 min). Pillar: Prophet stories (Tuesday long-form). Faithful, deeply sourced retelling of Surah Yusuf. Every claim is shown on screen with its source (foot): Qur'an by verse, hadith by collection (Sahih al-Bukhari 3390 lineage; Sahih al-Bukhari 3372 his patience in prison; Sahih Muslim 162 half of beauty), and the Conquest of Makkah from seerah. segments.text: It is the only chapter in the Qur'an that tells a single story from beginning to end. And Allah Himself calls it the most beautiful of stories.
And the Qur'an says something striking. Before this was revealed, the Prophet, peace be upon him, knew nothing of it. It reached him only from above.

Yusuf was born into a house unlike any other. The Prophet, peace be upon him, called him the noble, son of the noble, son of the noble, son of the noble: Yusuf, son of Yaqub, son of Ishaq, son of Ibrahim. Four prophets, one after another.
And it begins with a boy and a dream. Young Yusuf comes to his father one morning and tells him what he saw in his sleep.
O my father, I saw eleven stars, and the sun and the moon. I saw them prostrating to me.
His father knew at once the boy had been chosen for something great. And he warned him: do not tell this dream to your brothers.
Because they were already jealous. They saw how their father loved him, and they could not bear it. So they made a plan to be rid of their own brother.
They took him out to play, and threw him down into the darkness of a deep well. A child, alone, at the bottom of a pit.
Then they came to their father in the night, weeping false tears, holding out a shirt soaked in blood that was not Yusuf's. A wolf, they said, had devoured him.
Yaqub knew they were lying. And a prophet's heart, breaking, chose patience.
So beautiful patience is most fitting. And it is Allah whose help is sought against what you describe.
A passing caravan pulled the boy from the well and carried him down to Egypt, where they sold him for a few coins, as if he were worth nothing.
But Allah had placed him in the house of one of the most powerful men in Egypt. Thus, the Qur'an says, We established Yusuf in the land, and most people simply do not know.
He grew into a young man of extraordinary beauty. On the night of the Ascension, the Prophet, peace be upon him, met him, and said that Yusuf had been given half of all beauty.
And there came his hardest test. The wife of the master desired him. The doors were locked. No one would ever know.
And Yusuf refused, with the words of a man who feared his Lord far more than he feared any man: I seek refuge in Allah.

The women of the city mocked her for it. So she gathered them, gave them knives and fruit, and called him out before them. They were so overcome they cut their own hands. And she admitted what she wanted, and threatened him with prison.
And Yusuf made a choice almost no one would. He prayed that prison was dearer to him than the sin they were calling him to.
And so, though the signs of his innocence were plain, the young man was thrown into prison for years.
Two young men were jailed with him, each troubled by a dream. But before he read a single one, Yusuf turned them to something far greater.
O my two companions of prison, are many differing lords better, or Allah, the One, the Prevailing?
Then he told them the truth of their dreams: one would be freed to serve his king again, the other would not survive. And he asked the one who would be saved to remember him to his master.
Years passed. Then the king of Egypt saw a dream that no one in his court could explain. Seven fat cows devoured by seven lean ones. Seven green ears of grain, and seven dry.
They finally remembered the man in the prison. Yusuf read it for them: seven years of plenty are coming, then seven of famine. So store your grain, and prepare.

And here Yusuf did something extraordinary. When the king summoned him, he did not rush from his cell. He asked first that his name be cleared, that the women be questioned, so he would leave not as an accused man, but as an innocent one.
The Prophet, peace be upon him, marveled at that patience. He said that had he himself waited in prison as long as Yusuf, and then been called, he would have hurried straight out.
His innocence was proven. And Yusuf, once a slave and a prisoner, was placed in charge of the storehouses of all of Egypt.
And when the seven years of famine came, it spread far beyond Egypt, all the way back to the land of his father, and the brothers who had thrown him away.
They came to Egypt, begging for food. They stood before the most powerful man in the land and did not recognise the brother they had buried in a well.
He tested them, and by a plan Allah taught him, he kept his youngest brother at his side: the king's own cup, hidden in the boy's bag.
Back in Canaan, the loss broke the old man all over again. He wept for Yusuf until his eyes turned white with grief.
I only complain of my deep grief and sorrow to Allah.
And still, he told his sons: go back, and never lose hope in the mercy of Allah.
When they returned and stood before him once more, broken and desperate, the moment finally came. He said to them: I am Yusuf.
They froze, completely in his power, knowing exactly what they had done to him, and what he could now do to them.
He said: there is no blame upon you today. May Allah forgive you, and He is the most merciful of the merciful.
Centuries later, the Prophet, peace be upon him, would stand over the people of Makkah who had driven him out and tortured his companions, and echo these very words: Go, for you are free.
Yusuf sent his shirt to be laid over his father's face. And far away, before it had even arrived, the old man lifted his head: I sense the scent of Yusuf, though you will think I have lost my mind.

The shirt was laid over his eyes, and his sight returned. And his parents and his eleven brothers bowed before him in honour. The dream of the boy, the eleven stars, the sun and the moon, had finally come true.
You are my protector in this world and the next. Let me die in submission to You, and join me with the righteous.

He was betrayed by his own blood, enslaved, lied about, and imprisoned for years. And every single step was quietly carrying him exactly where Allah meant him to be. So trust the One who is writing your story too.

sources: Qur'an: Surah Yusuf (chapter 12), text from Quran.com. Verses shown on screen with translation, never recited or synthesized: 12:4, 12:18, 12:39, 12:86, 12:92, 12:101 (plus 12:5, 12:15-18, 12:21, 12:23, 12:31-35, 12:41-50, 12:54-58, 12:70-76, 12:84, 12:87, 12:93-100 cited per beat in the foot line).

Hadith (authentic): Sahih al-Bukhari 3390 (Yusuf's lineage of four prophets: the noble son of the noble son of the noble son of the noble); Sahih al-Bukhari 3372 (the Prophet on Yusuf's patience: had he waited in prison as long as Yusuf, he would have answered the call at once); Sahih Muslim 162 (on the night journey, Yusuf had been given half of all beauty).

Seerah: at the Conquest of Makkah the Prophet, peace be upon him, forgave the Quraysh with 'Go, for you are free', echoing Yusuf's 'no blame upon you today' (12:92) - recorded in the books of seerah. No Israiliyyat or weak narrations are used; no embellishment beyond the Qur'anic and authentic hadith record. Visuals are real Pexels footage (human-free, text-free, frame-grab QC'd) except the code-generated Canaan->Egypt map.
